metrics.narrative: No quantified business case articulated. Edgar stated the buyer is a $2.3B publicly traded organization 'big enough' for an eSourcing solution and named two priorities: sourcing events and spend visibility ('pull data more easily'). Alex acknowledged doing 'a great job getting a lot of the data' but implied current manual effort. No cost, headcount, cycle time, error rate, savings target, or ROI discussed. Buyer framed the need qualitatively ('wanna make a difference', 'get to a place where we can just pull that more easily') with no quantified pain or financial impact. Without a quantified value case, deal lacks exec-level business justification and CFO sponsorship lever. Next move: co-build a quantified ROI model with Edgar/Suzanne anchored to current sourcing-event cycle time, manual effort hours, and spend-visibility gaps, and present to CFO/finance.

competition.narrative: Primary competitor is Do Nothing / Status Quo. Buyer has no eSourcing tool today and relies on manual/ad-hoc RFx execution plus SAP standard tools and custom BI reports for spend visibility and PO tracking (Raj, discovery call 30 April). Edgar stated Simpson has been distracted by 8-year SAP implementation and is 'now at a point we wanna tackle this', but timeline is exploratory ('investigating this calendar year') with no decision urgency, compelling event, or budget allocated. Buyer has not named any vendor competitors (no Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP, Zip, Jaggaer mentioned). No RFP, RFI, or vendor shortlist discussed. Competitive risk is high: without exec sponsorship, quantified ROI, or urgency, status quo inertia (continue with SAP + manual processes) is the likely outcome. How we win vs status quo: co-build quantified savings/efficiency case with Edgar/Suzanne, secure CFO/finance sponsorship to validate ROI and budget, and create urgency through compelling event (FY27 budget lock, sourcing-event pipeline, peer benchmark).

value.flag: Quantify the business case and tie to a compelling event. Buyer articulated qualitative pain (manual sourcing events, poor spend visibility post-8-year SAP implementation) but provided no cost, savings target, cycle-time, or ROI. Zycus presented a 5-15% savings framework but buyer did not validate internal targets or link to budget cycle, contract expiration, or strategic initiative. Without a quantified, finance-validated business case and a compelling event (e.g. FY27 budget approval, procurement transformation tied to post-SAP optimization), the deal lacks urgency and executive sponsorship will not materialize. Recommended action: In follow-up meeting, co-create a business-case worksheet with Edgar/Suzanne tying Zycus ROI model to Simpson's indirect spend (estimate $200M+ at $2.3B revenue, ~10% indirect), quantify sourcing-event cycle-time savings, and map to a forcing function (e.g. 'secure FY27 budget approval by October 2026 board meeting' or 'complete procurement-transformation Phase 1 by Q1 2027'). Engage CFO (Magstadt) or SVP Finance (Swartzendruber) to validate ROI and secure executive sponsorship.
